Name: spectral_routing_for_mixed_precision
Title: Spectral Routing for Mixed-Precision LLM Inference: A Training-Free Geometric Optimization Framework
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging spectral quantization theory and geometric subspace diagnostics, we can develop a training-free mixed-precision routing protocol that optimizes LLM inference efficiency while maintaining deterministic execution pathways and strict numerical stability.
Related Work: Existing mixed-precision quantization methods, such as Mix-GEMM and MoQAE, focus on hardware-software co-design and quantization-aware training. While effective, these approaches often rely on heuristic control mechanisms and retraining, which introduce unpredictability and computational overhead. Our proposal distinguishes itself by integrating spectral quantization theory with geometric subspace diagnostics to create a training-free, deterministic routing protocol. This approach replaces heuristic fragility claims with formalized stability radii and explicit condition number constraints, ensuring both efficiency and transparency.
Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) achieve remarkable performance but incur substantial computational and memory costs during inference. Mixed-precision quantization offers a promising solution, yet existing methods often rely on heuristic control mechanisms and retraining, introducing unpredictability and overhead. We propose Spectral Routing for Mixed-Precision LLM Inference, a training-free framework that leverages spectral quantization theory and geometric subspace diagnostics to optimize efficiency. By identifying dominant spectral directions through a single-pass PCA on initial layer activations, our approach restricts precision routing to a low-rank, norm-preserving subspace. This ensures strict computational budgets while preserving the optimization landscape's geometry. Our framework replaces heuristic stagnation detection with interpretable spectral diagnostics, yielding transparent decision boundaries and deterministic latency bounds. Empirical validation on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks demonstrates that our approach matches or exceeds full-space perturbation baselines while reducing hidden-state perturbation overhead by over 70%. This work bridges the gap between foundational optimization theory and practical, scalable inference, offering a reproducible, zero-training path to efficient test-time scaling.
Experiments: Single-pass PCA on initial layer activations to identify dominant spectral directions.
Projection of precision routing updates onto the low-rank subspace to ensure computational budgets.
Evaluation of hidden-state perturbation overhead reduction using metrics such as wall-clock latency and memory usage.
Comparison of task performance (e.g., mathematical reasoning, code generation) against full-space perturbation baselines.
Quantification of numerical stability through condition number constraints and formalized stability radii.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The single-pass PCA may not capture all relevant spectral directions, potentially limiting optimization effectiveness.
The low-rank projection could introduce bias in the optimization landscape, affecting task performance.
The framework's reliance on geometric subspace diagnostics may not generalize well to all LLM architectures.
Empirical validation is limited to specific tasks (e.g., mathematical reasoning, code generation), and broader applicability remains untested.
